pricing: Tiered: a free Starter Kit (with higher ~3.09% + 15¢ in-person processing), a Point of Sale plan at $69/mo (~2.49% + 15¢), and Build Your Own from ~$110/mo; proprietary hardware runs $799+ per terminal, and online orders process at ~3.50% + 15¢. Real-world single-location cost is usually $150–$500/mo in software plus processing, and add-ons like online ordering, loyalty, and KDS run $50–$165/mo each.
bestFor: Operators who want one connected restaurant OS instead of a stack of disconnected tools, and don't mind committing to Toast's hardware and processing.
pros: All-in-one: POS, online ordering, KDS, payroll, loyalty under one roof
Deep restaurant-specific features and a huge integration ecosystem
Android handhelds built for tableside ordering and speed
Capital/financing and 0% hardware financing options ease upfront cost
cons: Proprietary hardware and processing lock-in
All-in monthly cost often climbs past $1,000 once add-ons stack
Add-on pricing and onboarding fees (~$95/hr) add up fast
Stuck on Toast's payment rates
integrations: Massive native integration marketplace plus first-party online ordering, KDS, payroll, loyalty; most third-party delivery and back-office tools connect.
verdict: The most complete all-in-one restaurant platform, and a strong pick if you'll use the whole stack — just budget honestly for hardware, processing, and add-ons, and accept the lock-in.

pricing: Three plans: Free ($0/mo), Plus ($49/mo per location), and Premium ($149/mo per location), all on iPad; processing rates step down on higher tiers and hardware is separate. No long-term contract, with free trials on paid plans.
bestFor: Single and small-multi independents who want an affordable, easy POS that just works and can start at $0/mo.
pros: Genuinely free entry tier with no monthly fee
Easy to set up and run; clean iPad interface
Tightly unified with Square payments, online ordering, and ecosystem
Predictable, transparent flat-rate processing
cons: Locked to Square payment processing
Thinner for complex coursing and large fine-dining floors
Advanced inventory/reporting gated behind paid tiers
Less restaurant-specialized than Toast or TouchBistro
integrations: Native to the Square ecosystem (Square Online, Loyalty, Payroll, KDS); growing third-party app marketplace, though narrower than Toast's.
verdict: The best low-risk starting POS for independents — affordable, friendly, and reliable, as long as you're fine processing payments through Square.

pricing: Cloud POS roughly $69/mo (Starter) up to $189/mo (Essential) and $399/mo (Premium), plus integrated payments; Enterprise is custom. Third-party sources cite ~$59/mo per added register, though Lightspeed doesn't publish that figure.
bestFor: Serious full-service and multi-location operators who need deep inventory, reporting, and analytics.
pros: Strong inventory management and recipe/cost tracking
Robust reporting and multi-location tools
Floor plans, table management, and coursing for full-service
All tiers bundle online ordering, loyalty, and contactless
cons: Premium tier is pricey for a tiny single shop
Per-register costs add up across stations
Steeper learning curve than Square
Best value tied to using Lightspeed Payments
integrations: Open API plus an app marketplace; integrates with accounting, delivery aggregators, reservations, and loyalty tools.
verdict: A powerful choice when inventory and reporting depth matter most; arguably overkill (and over-budget) for a simple single-location café.

pricing: Two paths: a $0/mo All-In plan (~2.79% + 20¢ card-present, 2-year minimum term) or POS Essentials at ~$55/mo per station (lower ~2.45% + 15¢ processing, month-to-month), plus ~$3/mo per employee and a ~$75/mo minimum. Switching processors triggers a $995 conversion fee and doubles the software license cost.
bestFor: Independents who want POS bundled with guest marketing, loyalty, and reviews — and value strong human support.
pros: POS bundled with marketing, loyalty, and reviews
Strong, well-reviewed customer support
Month-to-month option on Essentials
Competitive processing rates on the paid plan
cons: Must use SpotOn hardware and processing
All-In plan carries a 2-year minimum term
Steep penalties if you switch processors later
Per-employee and minimum fees nudge cost up
integrations: Native SpotOn marketing, loyalty, reservations, and online ordering; integrates with common accounting and delivery tools, though the ecosystem is smaller than Toast's.
verdict: A solid value when you want POS plus guest-marketing in one with real support — just go in clear-eyed about the contract terms and processor lock-in.

pricing: iPad POS bundle from $69/mo; an Essentials bundle at ~$119/mo adds integrated payments and hardware with $0 upfront. Add-ons (KDS, inventory, online ordering, loyalty, reservations) are quote-only — TouchBistro no longer publishes their prices.
bestFor: Full-service and fine-dining venues that want a POS purpose-built for tableside service, coursing, and floor management.
pros: Designed specifically for tableside full-service
Strong coursing, table, and floor management
No commission on its own online orders
Works offline (local/hybrid setup)
cons: Add-ons are quote-only and inflate the real cost
Weaker fit for high-volume QSR
Less transparent pricing than rivals
Smaller integration ecosystem
integrations: Integrates with accounting, payroll, and delivery tools; first-party reservations, online ordering, loyalty, and KDS as paid add-ons.
verdict: A purpose-built full-service POS that shines tableside; just press for full add-on pricing before signing, since the headline $69 rarely reflects the real bill.

pricing: Sold via banks and resellers, so pricing varies widely: software roughly $14–$90/mo per device, with restaurant plans cited around $135/mo (quick-service) to $179/mo (full-service), and hardware from ~$1,699 upfront. Card-present processing typically ~2.3–3.5% + 10¢, and most plans carry 36-month contracts with early-termination fees.
bestFor: Operators wanting flexible, customizable POS hardware with a big app store, who are comfortable shopping resellers for a good rate.
pros: Flexible hardware lineup and large app marketplace
Affordable entry software tiers
Widely supported and easy to source
Customizable to many service styles
cons: Reseller pricing and rates vary wildly — easy to overpay
Common 36-month contracts with steep early-termination fees
Processing rates depend on your merchant account
Less restaurant-native than Toast or TouchBistro
integrations: Large Clover app marketplace; integrates with accounting, online ordering, loyalty, and delivery tools — depth depends on apps you add.
verdict: Flexible and affordable on paper, but the value hinges entirely on the reseller deal and contract — read the fine print and compare processing rates carefully.

pricing: iPad POS from ~$99/terminal/mo (billed annually on a 3-year Revel Advantage contract, 2-terminal minimum), with implementation packages from ~$674 and ~2.49% processing via Revel Advantage. Contracts often auto-renew and early-termination fees can run into the thousands.
bestFor: High-volume and multi-unit QSR or chains that need a robust POS built for throughput.
pros: Built for high volume and many locations
Strong inventory, CRM, and reporting in the base
Scales across multi-unit operations
Mature, established platform
cons: 3-year contract plus 2-terminal minimum
High setup/implementation fees
Auto-renewal and large early-termination fees
Overkill and over-budget for a single small shop
integrations: Open API plus partner integrations for accounting, online ordering, loyalty, and delivery; tied to Revel Advantage processing for best pricing.
verdict: A capable POS for volume and scale, but the 3-year contract, minimums, and setup costs make it a poor fit for independents — best reserved for growing chains.

pricing: Commission-free model: subscription roughly $149–$199/mo per location plus standard ~2.95% + 29¢ processing, with a one-time setup fee (often $119–$499). Month-to-month, annual, and 2-year contract options; delivery via Flex is a flat per-order fee.
bestFor: Independents who want a branded, commission-free ordering channel to escape the 15–30% delivery-app cut.
pros: 0% commission on direct orders — keeps your margin
Branded ordering page plus a marketplace app
Marketing tools to drive repeat orders
Flexible contract terms available
cons: Subscription owed even in slow months
Processing and setup fees on top of the monthly
Less of a full website/brand builder than BentoBox
Pays off best at steady direct-order volume
integrations: Integrates with major POS systems (Toast, Square, Clover, and others) and pushes orders into the kitchen; connects to delivery dispatch.
verdict: A proven, restaurant-focused way to own commission-free ordering — the flat fee is well worth it once you're driving meaningful direct volume.

pricing: Two month-to-month plans: Flex at $249/mo plus a 5% per-order fee, or Flat-Rate at $499/mo with 0% restaurant commission; guests pay a 5% order-support fee. No contracts; both plans include website, app, ordering, loyalty, and marketing.
bestFor: Independents serious about winning commission-free repeat orders who do (or want to do) meaningful direct online volume.
pros: AI-built website, branded app, ordering, loyalty, and marketing in one
Commission-free on the Flat-Rate plan
No long-term contract; cancel anytime
Built specifically to grow direct repeat orders
cons: Flat $499 is steep at low order volume
Flex plan's 5% fee plus guest support fee adds up
5% guest-facing fee can deter some customers
Best ROI only above ~$5k/mo in online sales
integrations: POS integrations plus first-party website, app, loyalty, email/SMS, and SEO tooling; chargeback protection built in.
verdict: A strong all-in-one for independents focused on direct ordering — worth it once your online volume justifies the flat fee; otherwise start on the Flex plan.

pricing: Free site builder ($0/mo) with pickup/delivery ordering on Square; paid plans (roughly $29–$79+/mo) remove ads and add features, and you pay standard Square processing per order. No commission on direct orders.
bestFor: Square users and budget-conscious independents who want a zero-cost way to launch commission-free online ordering.
pros: Free to start with no commission on direct orders
Seamless if you already run Square POS
Quick to set up; syncs menu and payments automatically
Pickup, delivery, and curbside built in
cons: Generic commerce tool, lighter on restaurant-specific upsell
Locked to Square processing
Fewer marketing/brand features than BentoBox or Owner
Design flexibility is limited
integrations: Native to Square POS, Loyalty, and Payments; on-demand delivery via Square's delivery partners.
verdict: The cheapest credible path to commission-free ordering, especially for existing Square users — just don't expect the brand polish of a premium restaurant platform.

pricing: Website plans from ~$119/mo, with popular packages around $279/mo (Foundations) and $479/mo (Signature); Takeout & Delivery adds ~$49/mo plus ~$0.99/order and 3% processing, and Online Catering ~$79/mo. Month-to-month; total cost scales with order volume.
bestFor: Full-service and fine-dining brands that want the best-looking restaurant website with ordering and catering built in.
pros: Premium, design-forward restaurant websites and CMS
Commission-free ordering and strong catering tools
Brand and content control, not just a menu page
No long-term contract
cons: Per-order and processing fees stack on top of subscription
Strength is website/brand, not high-volume ordering
Total cost climbs quickly at higher order counts
Pricier than lean ordering-only tools
integrations: Integrates with major POS and reservation systems; first-party ordering, catering, events, and email marketing.
verdict: The pick when your website and brand are the priority and ordering is secondary — beautiful and capable, but watch the per-order fees as volume grows.

pricing: Built for pizzerias: roughly $39/mo plus ~$3.00 per online order (credit-card orders), commission-free versus the 20–30% delivery apps take. Pricing centers on the flat per-order model rather than a percentage commission.
bestFor: Independent pizzerias wanting low-cost, commission-free online ordering and marketing tailored to pizza.
pros: Purpose-built for pizza shops
Very low cost versus third-party commissions
Flat per-order fee is predictable
Marketing and marketplace exposure for pizzerias
cons: Pizza-only — not for other concepts
Per-order fees add up at high volume
Narrower feature set than full ordering platforms
Marketplace exposure can blur direct-brand ownership
integrations: Integrates with select pizzeria POS systems and offers its own ordering site/app and marketplace; narrower POS coverage than ChowNow.
verdict: If you run a pizzeria, Slice is one of the cheapest and most tailored ways to take commission-free orders — irrelevant for any other concept.

pricing: Enterprise SaaS with custom, quote-only pricing and no free plan; cost scales by location count and modules (ordering, dispatch, channel management). Widely reported as expensive and aimed at multi-location brands, not single independents.
bestFor: Large multi-unit and enterprise brands that need digital ordering, delivery dispatch, and channel management across many locations.
pros: Powers ordering across web, app, and aggregators at scale
Robust delivery dispatch and channel management
Enterprise-grade reliability and integrations
Centralized menu and data across locations
cons: Quote-only pricing; not transparent
Built for chains — overkill for one independent
Expensive for small operators
Implementation is involved
integrations: Deep POS and aggregator integrations (DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub), plus loyalty, CRM, and dispatch partners — a central hub for enterprise digital ordering.
verdict: The enterprise standard for multi-channel digital ordering at scale — powerful for chains, but the wrong tool (and budget) for a single independent restaurant.

pricing: Three plans: Basic ~$149/mo, Core ~$299/mo, Pro ~$499/mo, plus per-cover network fees (~$1–$1.50) and, as of early 2026, a 2% service fee on deposits/prepaid transactions. Website reservations are free on Core/Pro; Basic charges $0.25/cover or a $49/mo flat fee.
bestFor: Full-service and fine-dining restaurants that want maximum diner discovery on the largest US reservation network.
pros: Largest US diner network and discovery reach
Deposits and prepay options cut no-shows
Mature table and waitlist management
Strong guest data and reviews ecosystem
cons: Per-cover fees add up at volume
New 2% transaction fee on deposits/prepaid
Irrelevant for QSR/fast-casual
Network reach comes at a recurring cost premium
integrations: Integrates with major POS systems and many marketing/CRM tools; connects bookings from OpenTable, Google, and partner channels.
verdict: Worth the per-cover cost if you want the biggest discovery network filling tables; if you book mostly from your own site, a flat-fee rival may save money.

pricing: Flat monthly tiers with no per-cover fees: Basic ~$249/mo, Pro ~$399/mo, Enterprise ~$899/mo; pricing is predictable regardless of reservation count (an Amex-owned platform).
bestFor: Higher-end, high-volume full-service and fine-dining venues that want strong reservations without per-cover fees.
pros: Flat pricing — no per-cover fees
Strong table and waitlist management
Trendy, urban diner audience and Amex perks
Predictable monthly cost beats per-cover at volume
cons: Higher entry price than OpenTable Basic
Skews trendy/urban; smaller reach in some markets
Weak fit for QSR/fast-casual
Discovery network smaller than OpenTable's
integrations: Integrates with major POS systems and marketing tools; event ticketing and guest management built in.
verdict: A strong reservations platform whose flat pricing wins once you exceed roughly 175 covers a month — best for buzzy, higher-volume restaurants.

pricing: Four tiers: Base ~$79/mo (waitlist/events, no reservations), Essential ~$199/mo (adds reservations and takeout), Premium ~$339/mo (POS integration, premium support), and Premium Unlimited ~$769/mo (removes prepayment fees); lower tiers carry a ~2–3% fee on prepaid transactions.
bestFor: Fine-dining and experience-driven venues that want prepaid deposits and ticketed events to crush no-shows.
pros: Deposits and prepaid experiences slash no-shows
Built-in event and ticketing tools
Flexible tiers from waitlist-only up to unlimited
Strong fit for tasting menus and special events
cons: Prepaid model is overkill for casual walk-in venues
Prepayment fees on lower tiers
Premium Unlimited is pricey
Smaller diner-discovery network than OpenTable
integrations: POS integration on Premium tiers, plus marketing and CRM connections; ticketing and prepay handled natively.
verdict: The best choice when deposits and prepaid experiences are central to protecting revenue — but unnecessary complexity for a casual, walk-in-driven spot.

pricing: Three editions: Basic ~$99/mo (up to 500 covers/mo), Plus ~$299/mo (unlimited covers, POS integration, custom floor plan), and Enterprise (custom); no per-cover fees. New customers can get up to 60 days free plus Yelp Ads credit.
bestFor: Fast-casual and full-service venues wanting reservations plus waitlist with wide discovery and no per-cover fees.
pros: No per-cover fees
Discovery across Yelp, Google, and Apple
Waitlist, two-way texting, and to-go management included
Generous intro offers and ads credit
cons: Tied to the Yelp ecosystem
POS integration and floor plans gated to Plus tier
Discovery network skews to Yelp users
Less prestige than OpenTable/Resy for fine dining
integrations: POS integration on the Plus plan; reservations and waitlist surface across Yelp, Google, and Apple Maps.
verdict: A solid, fee-light reservations and waitlist option with real discovery reach — especially attractive if your guests already find you on Yelp.

pricing: Custom, quote-only pricing; operator reports put the core platform around ~$499/mo per venue on an annual contract, with add-ons like WhatsApp messaging (~$100/mo) pushing a full single-venue setup to ~$600–$900/mo. Multi-year TCO commonly runs into the tens of thousands.
bestFor: Full-service and fine-dining groups that want best-in-class guest data, CRM, and marketing alongside reservations.
pros: Best-in-class guest profiles and personalization
Reservations plus CRM, marketing, and loyalty in one
You own your guest data
Strong automation for repeat-visit marketing
cons: Pricey and complex for a single small venue
Quote-only pricing requires a sales process
Add-ons (e.g. WhatsApp) raise the real cost
Implementation and learning curve are significant
integrations: Deep POS integrations plus email/SMS marketing, payments, and loyalty; built as a guest-data hub across reservations and dining.
verdict: The strongest reservations-plus-CRM platform for groups that will actually use the guest data — but heavy and expensive for a lone small restaurant.

pricing: Free tier (1 location, up to ~50–100 visits/mo), Starter from ~$31/mo (up to 2,500 visits, up to 10 locations), Business from ~$55/mo (up to 5,000 visits), and custom Enterprise; annual billing saves ~20%.
bestFor: Walk-in-heavy QSR and fast-casual venues that need a simple, cheap waitlist and queue system rather than a diner network.
pros: Free tier and very low entry price
Simple waitlist and queue management
Two-way SMS and self-check-in
Scales to multiple locations affordably
cons: Not a diner-network reservation platform
No discovery/marketing reach like OpenTable
Visit caps on lower tiers
Lighter on full-service table management
integrations: API access on higher tiers plus integrations with common business tools; not a deep restaurant-POS reservations hub.
verdict: A cheap, no-nonsense way to tame the door line for walk-in venues — just don't expect it to fill seats the way a diner-network platform does.

pricing: Flat monthly subscription: Core from ~$399/location/mo and Premium from ~$599/location/mo, with a custom enterprise tier; reported real-world cost runs ~$450–$600/location/mo. Unlimited call handling regardless of volume; multi-location groups can negotiate.
bestFor: Full-service and fine-dining venues that lose booking and FAQ calls during the rush and want a polished 24/7 voice answer.
pros: Answers calls 24/7 and books reservations
Handles FAQs, special requests, and concierge bypass
Reservation integrations (OpenTable, SevenRooms, Yelp)
Flat pricing regardless of call volume
cons: Texts an order link rather than taking phone orders
Premium integrations/analytics cost more
$399+ floor is steep for very low call volume
Focused on bookings/FAQs more than ordering
integrations: Integrates with OpenTable, SevenRooms, and Yelp reservations; sends order links rather than injecting orders into a POS.
verdict: A strong, polished AI answer for reservation- and FAQ-heavy restaurants — but if you need the phone to actually take orders, look at an ordering-first agent.

pricing: Monthly subscription from ~$199/mo; reported tiers include a Standard plan around $299/mo (up to ~500 minutes, one location) and Premium around $529/mo (up to ~1,000 minutes, up to 3 locations, voice cloning). Custom-quoted by team size and needs; one of the lower-priced order-taking agents.
bestFor: Independents and small groups that want the phone to actually take orders, reservations, and payment around the clock.
pros: Takes phone orders, reservations, and payment
Lower-priced than most order-taking AI agents
Handles unlimited simultaneous calls
Built-in upsell engine to lift ticket size
cons: Base plans have minute caps
Younger vendor with a shorter track record
POS integration depth varies (falls back to fax/email)
Custom pricing requires a quote
integrations: Syncs orders/reservations to supported POS systems where available, with fax/email fallback when direct integration isn't possible.
verdict: One of the most affordable ways to capture phone orders 24/7 — a strong pick for order-heavy independents, just confirm POS integration for your system.

pricing: Conversation-based tiers: Free (1 agent, ~20 phone/40 text conversations/mo), Small ~$99/mo (~60 phone/120 text), Medium ~$299/mo (~200 phone/400 text), Large ~$499/mo (~400 phone/800 text), Corp ~$799/mo (~800 phone/1,600 text), plus custom Enterprise.
bestFor: Restaurants wanting a flexible voice-and-text agent across calls, reservations, and delivery/catering, with a low-cost or free entry point.
pros: Free tier to trial it
Handles both voice and text channels
Covers reservations, delivery, and catering workflows
Affordable scaling tiers
cons: Conversation caps on every tier
Horizontal product, less restaurant-specialized
Higher volume needs the pricier tiers
Newer, less restaurant-proven than some rivals
integrations: CRM integration on higher tiers plus food-delivery workflows; restaurant-POS integration depth is lighter than ordering-first specialists.
verdict: A flexible, affordable voice-plus-text agent with a free trial tier — good for varied use cases, though its caps and horizontal focus may not suit high call volume.

pricing: AI receptionist tiers: Starter ~$79/mo, Growth ~$129/mo, Scale ~$249/mo (monthly billing; ~17% off annually), all with unlimited minutes and a 14-day trial. Overage runs ~$0.50 per unique caller above the plan cap.
bestFor: Small operators who simply want to stop sending callers to voicemail at the lowest possible cost.
pros: Cheapest entry point of the AI-phone tools
Unlimited minutes on all plans
Books appointments and sends SMS follow-ups
14-day free trial
cons: Per-unique-caller overage can balloon at volume
Not restaurant-native (general service-business tool)
Lighter on POS/ordering integration
Less restaurant-specific reservation handling
integrations: Connects to calendars and CRMs and uses configurable skill flows; not a restaurant-POS-native ordering tool.
verdict: The budget way to stop missing calls, fine for FAQs and basic booking — but its per-caller overage and non-restaurant focus limit it for high-volume order-taking.

pricing: Pricing not public — quote only; enterprise/brand-level deals scaled per location and lane. (Existing estimate ~$300–500/mo/loc is illustrative only — confirm on a live quote.)
bestFor: QSR and fast-casual chains that want fully autonomous GenAI voice ordering in the drive-thru lane.
pros: Fully autonomous GenAI voice ordering, no human in the loop
Upsells on ~100% of orders to lift ticket size
Omnichannel: drive-thru, call, text, scan, in-car, kiosk
Smart-lane pre-ordering to boost throughput
cons: Quote-only, enterprise pricing
Built for brands/chains — overkill for one store
Requires POS integration and rollout effort
Not aimed at full-service or single independents
integrations: POS-integrated voice ordering across drive-thru and other channels; part of SoundHound's broader restaurant ordering platform.
verdict: A leading autonomous drive-thru voice solution for chains chasing throughput and consistent upsell — pricing and scale put it out of reach for single independents.

pricing: Pricing not public — quote only (demo required); reportedly no upfront implementation cost, but built for enterprise QSR budgets. (Existing estimate ~$750–2,000/mo/loc is illustrative only — confirm on a live quote.)
bestFor: Large QSR chains and ghost kitchens needing voice AI to absorb peak phone and drive-thru ordering volume.
pros: Proven at scale — 1,200+ stores across national brands
Handles phone and drive-thru ordering with upsell
Integrates with order-management platforms (e.g. Deliverect, Olo)
Frees staff during peak rushes
cons: Quote-only pricing
Built for large chains, not single sites
Implementation can take months
Higher budget required than single-store tools
integrations: Integrates with order-management and POS platforms including Deliverect and Olo, flowing voice orders alongside delivery and digital channels.
verdict: A scale-proven voice-AI ordering platform for major QSR brands — capable and battle-tested, but the implementation and budget suit chains, not independents.

pricing: Pricing not public — quote only; deployed across ~1,000 stores for QSR chains. (Existing estimate ~$500–1,500/mo/loc is illustrative only — confirm on a live quote; comparable rivals advertise around $475/mo.)
bestFor: QSR chains wanting accurate, high-throughput AI order-taking in the drive-thru and on the phone.
pros: High accuracy (~96%) and order completion (~93%+) at scale
Purpose-built for noisy drive-thru environments
Bilingual English/Spanish support
Optimized upselling and labor efficiency
cons: Quote-only pricing
Chain-focused; limited fit for full-service or single sites
Requires POS/drive-thru hardware integration
Rollout effort across locations
integrations: POS-integrated drive-thru and phone ordering; designed for franchise/multi-unit deployment with location-level flexibility.
verdict: A drive-thru voice specialist with strong accuracy at scale — a good chain choice, but the quote-only enterprise model isn't built for a single independent.

pricing: Tiered subscription: from ~$179/mo (or ~$159/mo annual) up to Essentials ~$299/mo and Premier ~$499/mo, plus add-ons — AI marketing ~$300/mo, online ordering ~$50/mo (+~$1/order to the guest), and ~$300/mo per extra location. SMS volume is capped by tier.
bestFor: Independents and small groups wanting an engaging interactive menu plus AI phone/text answering and marketing in one bundle.
pros: Interactive menu plus AI phone/text answering
Built-in marketing: email, SMS, reviews, promotions
Website and digital menus included
All-in-one guest engagement suite
cons: Marketing-first, not ordering-first (ordering is an add-on)
Add-ons (AI marketing, ordering, extra locations) stack the cost
SMS messages capped by plan
Hard to buy just one piece a la carte
integrations: Integrates with major POS systems and online-ordering/delivery tools; first-party website, menus, AI answering, and marketing.
verdict: A capable engagement bundle if you'll use the interactive menu plus marketing and AI answering together — just price the add-ons, since the base plan is only the start.

pricing: Subscription from roughly $64–$80/mo with a free trial; now a Deliverect company, so it's strongest as part of the Deliverect ecosystem. Turns Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp into ordering channels plus QR dine-in pay.
bestFor: Social-led QSR, fast-casual, and ghost kitchens (especially those already on Deliverect) wanting to take orders directly in social chat.
pros: Ordering via Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp
Centralized commerce inbox across social channels
QR dine-in ordering and pay
Low entry price with a free trial
cons: Strongest in EMEA markets
Best value if you're already on Deliverect
Less established in the US than rivals
Narrower than a full website/ordering platform
integrations: Deep Deliverect ecosystem integration (POS and aggregator sync) plus Meta channels — Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp.
verdict: A clever, affordable way to turn your social channels into ordering channels — most compelling if you already run Deliverect or sell heavily through Instagram/WhatsApp.

pricing: Pricing not public — enterprise, quote-only, scaled per location across a platform powering 40,000+ restaurants. (Existing note 'enterprise per loc' stands — confirm on a live quote.)
bestFor: High-volume QSR and chains wanting omnichannel ordering across web, app, kiosk, and call-center from one platform.
pros: Omnichannel ordering: web, app, kiosk, delivery, call-center
AI recommendations that lift check size (kiosks +15–30%)
Integrated loyalty, coupons, and enterprise tools
Connects POS, delivery, CRM, and CDP for one view
cons: Enterprise/chain-focused; overkill for single sites
Quote-only pricing
Implementation is involved
Not aimed at independents or full-service
integrations: Connects to POS, delivery service providers, CRM, CDP, and third-party delivery apps as a central enterprise ordering hub.
verdict: A strong omnichannel ordering platform for large brands chasing kiosk and app upsell — powerful at scale, but the wrong fit and budget for an independent restaurant.
